name: variable importance via permutation
description: A method in random forests to measure variable importance by randomly permuting values of a variable in out-of-bag samples and measuring the decrease in prediction accuracy.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: proximity plot
description: A two-dimensional visualization of the proximity matrix using multidimensional scaling, showing which observations are effectively close together according to the random forest classifier.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: de-correlation effect in random forests
description: The variance reduction mechanism in random forests achieved by reducing correlation between trees through random selection of input variables at each split.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: sampling correlation in random forests
description: The theoretical correlation ρ(x) between a pair of random-forest trees evaluated at x, induced by the sampling distribution of training data and randomization parameters.
section: 15.4.1 Variance and the De-Correlation Eﬀect

name: within-z variance
description: The variance component in random forests due to randomization conditional on a fixed training set, which increases as the number of randomly selected variables (m) decreases.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: random forest as adaptive nearest neighbors
description: An interpretation of random forests as a weighted k-nearest neighbor classifier where the tree-growing algorithm finds optimal paths to training observations and the averaging process assigns weights forming an equivalent kernel.
section: 15.4.3 Adaptive Nearest Neighbors

name: error-correcting output codes
description: An ensemble method for multiclass classification that uses a coding matrix to convert a K-class problem into L binary classification problems, where redundant bits allow for inaccuracies and can improve performance.
section: 16.1 Introduction

name: hamming distance
description: A distance metric between two vectors measuring the number of mismatches between corresponding entries.
section: 16.1 Introduction

name: forward stagewise linear regression
description: A regularization algorithm that starts with zero coefficients and at each iteration selects the basis function that best fits current residuals, incrementing its coefficient by an infinitesimal amount while leaving others unchanged.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: bet on sparsity principle
description: A principle for high-dimensional problems stating to use procedures that work well in sparse settings, since no procedure does well in dense problems where there are too many nonzero coefficients relative to sample size.
section: 16.2.2 The “Bet on Sparsity” Principle

name: monotone lasso
description: A variant of the lasso solved by infinitesimal forward stagewise regression, characterized by monotone nondecreasing coefficient paths in an augmented dictionary with non-negative coefficients.
section: 16.2.2 The “Bet on Sparsity” Principle

name: l1 normalized margin
description: A margin measure for classification models defined as the minimum over training samples of y·f(x) divided by the L1 norm of coefficients, measuring distance to closest training point in L∞ units.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: importance sampled learning ensemble
description: A hybrid ensemble learning approach that generates a finite dictionary of basis functions via randomized sub-sampling and then fits a lasso regularization path to select and weight the most relevant basis functions.
section: 16.3.1 Learning a Good Ensemble

name: characteristic width of sampling scheme
description: A measure σ of the randomness in an ensemble generation sampling scheme, defined as the expected increase in loss Q(γ) above the optimal value Q(γ*), balancing between too narrow (similar basis functions) and too wide (many irrelevant cases).
section: 16.3.1 Learning a Good Ensemble

name: rule ensemble
description: An ensemble method that extracts individual rules from each tree in a collection, creating an over-complete basis of simpler interpretable components that can be post-processed with lasso or other regularization.
section: 16.3.2 Rule Ensembles

name: markov random field
description: An undirected graphical model where vertices represent random variables and the absence of edges implies conditional independence given other variables, also known as Markov networks.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: pairwise markov independence
description: The property in Markov graphs that if two vertices are not adjacent, the corresponding random variables are conditionally independent given all other variables.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: global markov property
description: The property in Markov graphs that if subgraph C separates subgraphs A and B, then A and B are conditionally independent given C, allowing decomposition into smaller manageable pieces.
section: 17.2 Markov Graphs and Their Properties

name: clique in graph theory
description: A complete subgraph where all vertices are adjacent to one another; a maximal clique is one where no additional vertices can be added while maintaining the property.
section: 17.2 Markov Graphs and Their Properties

name: clique potential
description: Positive functions ψC(·) in the factorization of probability densities over Markov graphs that capture dependence in variables within a clique by scoring certain instances higher than others, though not necessarily being density functions themselves.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: hammersley-clifford theorem
description: A fundamental result stating that for Markov networks with positive distributions, the representation of probability density as a product of clique potentials is equivalent to the independence properties defined by the graph structure.
section: 15.1 Introduction

name: junction tree algorithm
description: An algorithm for computing marginal and low-order probabilities from joint distributions on graphs by decomposing the graph into maximal cliques and accumulating quantities across them, also known as join tree algorithm.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: pairwise markov graph
description: A graphical model with a potential function for each edge (pair of variables) and at most second-order interactions. More parsimonious than general graphical models and easier to work with.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: gaussian graphical model
description: A graphical model where all variables are continuous with multivariate Gaussian distribution. The inverse covariance matrix contains information about partial covariances between variables.
section: 17.3 Undirected Graphical Models for Continuous

name: inverse covariance matrix
description: The matrix Θ = Σ−1 that contains information about partial covariances. If the ijth component is zero, then variables i and j are conditionally independent given other variables.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: natural parameter for gaussian graphical model
description: The inverse covariance matrix Θ captures all second-order information (structural and quantitative) needed to describe the conditional distribution of each node given the rest.
section: 17.3.1 Estimation of the Parameters when the Graph

name: covariance graph
description: A graphical model where vertices are connected by bidirectional edges if the covariance (rather than partial covariance) between variables is nonzero. Also called a relevance network.
section: 17.3.1 Estimation of the Parameters when the Graph

name: relevance network
description: Alternative name for covariance graph, popular in genomics, where edges represent nonzero covariances rather than partial covariances.
section: 17.3.1 Estimation of the Parameters when the Graph

name: modified regression algorithm for graphical models
description: An iterative procedure that solves for Θ and its inverse W one row and column at a time by performing constrained regressions using the current covariance estimate.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: iterative proportional fitting procedure
description: A method for solving equality-constrained convex optimization problems in graphical model estimation where some edges are missing.
section: 17.3.1 Estimation of the Parameters when the Graph

name: positive definite completion
description: The process of estimating a complete covariance matrix from a partially specified one, subject to constraints from a graphical model structure.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: meinshausen-bühlmann approach
description: A simple approach to graphical model estimation that fits lasso regressions using each variable as response and others as predictors, estimating which components of θij are nonzero without fully estimating Σ or Θ.
section: 17.3.2 Estimation of the Graph Structure

name: soft-threshold operator
description: An operator S(x, t) = sign(x)(|x| − t)+ that shrinks values toward zero by thresholding, used in lasso and graphical lasso algorithms.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: pathwise coordinate descent for graphical lasso
description: An efficient algorithm that cycles through predictors, updating each using the soft-threshold operator until convergence, used to solve the modified lasso problem at each stage of graphical lasso.
section: 17.3.2 Estimation of the Graph Structure

name: ising model
description: An undirected Markov network with all discrete (typically binary) variables modeling pairwise interactions. The joint probability has the form Pr(X) = exp(ΣθjkXjXk - Φ(Θ)).
section: 17.4 Undirected Graphical Models for Discrete

name: boltzmann machine
description: A pairwise Markov network with binary variables where vertices (nodes/units) can be observed (visible) or unobserved (hidden), often organized in layers similar to a neural network.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: visible units
description: Nodes in a Boltzmann machine where the values are observed, as opposed to hidden units.
section: 17.4 Undirected Graphical Models for Discrete

name: hidden units in boltzmann machines
description: Nodes in a Boltzmann machine where the values are unobserved/latent, allowing the model to capture complex nonlinear relationships.
section: 17.4 Undirected Graphical Models for Discrete

name: poisson log-linear modeling for graphical models
description: Treating the Ising model estimation as a regression problem where the response is the vector of 2^p counts in each cell of the multiway tabulation, with computational cost O(p^4*2^p).
section: 17.4.1 Estimation of the Parameters when the Graph

name: mean field approximation
description: An approximation method that estimates EΘ(XjXk) by EΘ(Xj)EΘ(Xj) and replaces input variables by their means, leading to nonlinear equations for parameters θjk.
section: 17.4.2 Hidden Nodes

name: restricted boltzmann machine
description: A Boltzmann machine with one layer of visible units and one layer of hidden units with no connections within each layer, simplifying conditional expectation computations.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: contrastive divergence
description: A training method for RBMs where the second expectation in the gradient is estimated by running the Markov chain for only a few steps instead of to convergence, proposed by Hinton (2002).
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: greedy layer-by-layer learning
description: A method for learning features in deep networks by training RBMs sequentially, where hidden states from one RBM become visible units for the next.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: high-dimensional problem
description: A prediction problem where the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p >> N. High variance and overfitting are major concerns.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: diagonal-covariance lda
description: A version of LDA that assumes features are independent within each class (diagonal within-class covariance matrix), reducing parameters and often working well when p >> N.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: independence rule
description: Alternative name for diagonal-covariance LDA, demonstrated theoretically to often outperform standard LDA in high-dimensional settings.
section: 18.2 Nearest Shrunken Centroids

name: nearest shrunken centroids
description: A regularized nearest centroid classifier that shrinks classwise means toward the overall mean for each feature separately using soft thresholding, performing automatic feature selection.
section: 18.2 Nearest Shrunken Centroids

name: soft thresholding for nearest shrunken centroids
description: Shrinking standardized classwise differences toward zero using d'_kj = sign(d_kj)(|d_kj| - Δ)+, setting values to zero if their absolute value is less than threshold Δ.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: symmetric multiclass logistic model
description: A version of multiclass logistic regression where all K classes have their own coefficient vectors, with regularization forcing Σˆβ_lj = 0.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: one versus one approach
description: A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by computing all pairwise classifiers and using voting to determine the predicted class.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: one versus all approach
description: A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by comparing each class to all others in K two-class comparisons, choosing the class with highest confidence.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: computational shortcuts for p >> n
description: Using singular value decomposition to perform computations in N-dimensional space rather than p-dimensional when p > N, reducing cost from O(p^3) to O(pN^2).
section: 18.3.5 Computational Shortcuts When p ≫N

name: protein mass spectrometry
description: A technology for analyzing proteins in blood by observing intensity for many time of flight values, related to mass over charge ratio (m/z) of constituent proteins.
section: 18.4.1 Application of Lasso to Protein Mass Spectroscopy

name: fused lasso
description: A modification of the lasso for functional data that penalizes both the L1 norm of coefficients and the L1 norm of their successive differences, encouraging sparsity and smoothness.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: fused lasso signal approximator
description: A special case of fused lasso with X = I_N used to approximate a sequence, balancing fidelity to data with sparsity and smoothness.
section: 18.4.2 The Fused Lasso for Functional Data

name: string kernel
description: A similarity measure for variable-length strings based on counting matching substrings, computing inner products K(x1,x2) = Σφ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) where φ_a counts occurrences of substring a.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: bag of words representation
description: A document representation where features x_ij are counts of how many times word j appears in document i, discarding word order.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: double-centered kernel
description: Computing principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix by centering: (I - M)K(I - M) where M is the mean operator.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: supervised principal components
description: A method for high-dimensional regression that filters features by univariate significance, then computes principal components on the reduced set, and uses them in a regression model.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: cox proportional hazards model
description: A regression model widely used for survival problems, used in supervised principal components for score tests and multivariate modeling with censored outcomes.
section: 18.6.1 Connection to Latent-Variable Modeling

name: right censored survival data
description: Survival data where for some individuals we only know they lived at least until a certain time, not their exact survival time.
section: 18.6 High-Dimensional Regression: Supervised

name: kaplan-meier method
description: A method for estimating survival curves from censored survival data.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: latent variable model for supervised pca
description: A single-component factor analysis model where outcome Y relates to latent factor U, and relevant features X_j also relate to U through linear models.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: thresholded partial least squares
description: A variant of partial least squares where features are thresholded based on their correlation with the outcome before applying PLS, similar to supervised principal components.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: pre-conditioning for interpretability
description: A method for finding a reduced set of features that approximates a model when the threshold yields too many features for interpretation.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: pre-conditioned lasso
description: A variant of the lasso where the outcome variable is first denoised using supervised principal components, then the lasso is applied with the denoised outcome as the target. All features are used in the lasso fit, not just those retained in thresholding.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: two-sample t-statistic for genes
description: A standardized measure of differential expression between two groups for each gene, computed as the difference in mean expression divided by pooled standard error.
section: 18.7 Feature Assessment and the Multiple-Testing Problem

name: pooled within-group standard error
description: The standard error estimate for a gene computed by pooling variance estimates across both treatment groups.
section: 18.7 Feature Assessment and the Multiple-Testing Problem

name: permutation distribution for hypothesis testing
description: A nonparametric approach to computing p-values by permuting sample labels and computing test statistics for each permutation, avoiding distributional assumptions.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: pooled permutation p-values
description: P-values computed by pooling test statistic results across all genes rather than computing separate permutation distributions for each gene, yielding more granular p-values.
section: 18.7 Feature Assessment and the Multiple-Testing Problem

name: family-wise error rate
description: The probability of making at least one false rejection among all hypothesis tests, a conservative measure of overall error in multiple testing.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: bonferroni method
description: A multiple testing correction that controls family-wise error rate by testing each hypothesis at significance level α/M, where M is the number of tests.
section: 18.7 Feature Assessment and the Multiple-Testing Problem

name: false discovery rate
description: The expected proportion of false positives among all features called significant, defined as E[V/max(R,1)] where V is false positives and R is total rejections.
section: 18.7.1 The False Discovery Rate

name: benjamini-hochberg procedure
description: A procedure for controlling false discovery rate at level α by ordering p-values and finding the largest index where p(j) ≤ jα/M, then rejecting all hypotheses with smaller p-values.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: plug-in estimate of false discovery rate
description: A direct estimate of FDR computed as the average number of permutation values exceeding a threshold divided by the number of observed values exceeding that threshold.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: significance analysis of microarrays
description: A method for computing asymmetric significance cutpoints by plotting ordered test statistics against expected order statistics from permutations and finding where points leave a band of width Δ from the 45° line.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: asymmetric cutpoints in multiple testing
description: Using different significance thresholds for positive and negative test statistics when most differentially expressed features change in one direction.
section: 18.7.2 Asymmetric Cutpoints and the SAM Procedure

name: positive false discovery rate
description: A variant of FDR defined as E[V/R | R > 0], the expected false discovery rate conditional on making at least one rejection.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: mixture model for hypothesis testing
description: A Bayesian framework where each test statistic comes from either the null distribution F0 with probability π0 or the alternative distribution F1 with probability 1-π0.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: q-value
description: The smallest false discovery rate over all rejection regions that would reject a given test statistic, providing a local measure of significance for each feature.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: local false discovery rate
description: The positive FDR for an infinitesimal rejection region surrounding a specific test statistic value, providing the most local measure of significance.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: supervised principal components for feature selection
description: A method that thresholds features based on univariate association with outcome, then performs principal components analysis on retained features and uses components in supervised learning.
section: 18.6.3 Pre-Conditioning for Feature Selection

name: denoising the outcome variable
description: Using a first-stage predictor to reduce noise in the outcome before applying a second-stage method, reducing overfitting with many noisy features.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: feature assessment without prediction models
description: Assessing significance of individual features for scientific understanding rather than building multivariate predictive models, focusing on hypothesis testing.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: type-I error rate
description: The probability of falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis, typically set at α = 0.05 for individual tests.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: positive dependence between tests
description: A correlation structure where Pr(Aj|Ak) > Pr(Aj), meaning tests are more likely to reject together, which reduces family-wise error rate below the independent case.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: bh rejection threshold
description: The p-value cutoff p(L) determined by the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure, where L is the largest index satisfying p(j) ≤ jα/M.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: expected order statistics from permutations
description: The average of ordered test statistics across permutations, denoted ť(j) = (1/K)Σk tk(j), used as null reference in SAM procedure.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: sam tuning parameter delta
description: The width parameter Δ in SAM that defines parallel bands around the 45° line, controlling the distance at which genes are called significant.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: bayesian interpretation of fdr
description: Under a mixture model, the pFDR equals the posterior probability that the null hypothesis is true given the test statistic falls in the rejection region.
section: 18.8 Bibliographic Notes

name: radiation sensitivity gene expression study
description: A microarray study with 58 patient samples (44 normal, 14 radiation-sensitive) and 12,625 genes, used to identify genes associated with adverse radiation response.
section: 18.7 Feature Assessment and the Multiple-Testing Problem

name: diagonal linear discriminant analysis for high dimensions
description: A variant of LDA that uses a diagonal covariance matrix, appropriate when p >> N and features can be assumed independent.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: nearest shrunken centroids classifier
description: A classification method that shrinks class centroids toward the overall mean, effectively performing feature selection by setting some centroid components to zero.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N

name: elastic net
description: A regularization method combining L1 (lasso) and L2 (ridge) penalties, providing both feature selection and grouping of correlated features.
section: 18.1 When p is Much Bigger than N
